Step 1: Project Setup

# Create a new Next.js project with TypeScript and Tailwind
npx create-next-app@latest ai-assistant --typescript --tailwind --app
cd ai-assistant

# Install Vercel AI SDK and provider packages
npm install ai @ai-sdk/openai @ai-sdk/anthropic

# Install additional utilities
npm install zod react-markdown
Step 2: Environment Configuration

# .env.local
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
# Optional: for multi-provider support
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
Step 3: Basic Streaming Chat API Route (App Router)
The AI SDK provides a streamlined way to create streaming chat endpoints. Here's the modern approach using the AI SDK v3+ with `@ai-sdk/openai`.

// app/api/chat/route.ts
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { streamText } from 'ai';

export const maxDuration = 30; // Allow longer responses on Vercel

export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { messages } = await req.json();

  const result = streamText({
    model: openai('gpt-4o'),
    system: 'You are a helpful assistant. Be concise and accurate.',
    messages,
  });

  return result.toDataStreamResponse();
}
Step 4: Client-Side Chat Component with useChat Hook
The `useChat` hook manages message state, streaming, and form submission automatically.

// app/page.tsx
'use client';

import { useChat } from 'ai/react';
import ReactMarkdown from 'react-markdown';

export default function ChatPage() {
  const { messages, input, handleInputChange, handleSubmit, isLoading } = useChat({
    api: '/api/chat',
  });

  return (
    

      

        {messages.map((m) => (
          

            

              {m.role === 'user' ? 'You' : 'Assistant'}
            

            {m.content}
          

        ))}
        {isLoading && (
          

            
Assistant

            

              
              
              
            

          

        )}
      


      

        
        
      

    

  );
}
Advanced: Tool Calling with Vercel AI SDK
Tool calling allows the LLM to interact with external systems—querying databases, calling APIs, or even modifying application state. The AI SDK makes this straightforward.
Defining Tools on the Server

// app/api/chat/route.ts (with tools)
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { streamText, tool } from 'ai';
import { z } from 'zod';

export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { messages } = await req.json();

  const result = streamText({
    model: openai('gpt-4o'),
    system: 'You are a helpful assistant with access to weather data and a calculator.',
    messages,
    tools: {
      getWeather: tool({
        description: 'Get the current weather for a city',
        parameters: z.object({
          city: z.string().describe('The city name'),
          unit: z.enum(['celsius', 'fahrenheit']).optional().default('celsius'),
        }),
        execute: async ({ city, unit }) => {
          // In production, call a real weather API
          const temp = Math.floor(Math.random() * 30) + 5;
          return {
            city,
            temperature: unit === 'fahrenheit' ? (temp * 9/5 + 32) : temp,
            unit,
            condition: ['Sunny', 'Cloudy', 'Rainy'][Math.floor(Math.random() * 3)],
          };
        },
      }),
      calculate: tool({
        description: 'Evaluate a mathematical expression',
        parameters: z.object({
          expression: z.string().describe('The math expression to evaluate'),
        }),
        execute: async ({ expression }) => {
          try {
            // WARNING: eval is dangerous; use a proper math parser in production
            const result = eval(expression);
            return { expression, result };
          } catch (error) {
            return { error: 'Invalid expression' };
          }
        },
      }),
    },
  });

  return result.toDataStreamResponse();
}
Generating Structured Data with generateObject
For non-chat use cases—like extracting information, classification, or generating JSON—use `generateObject`.

// app/api/extract/route.ts
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { generateObject } from 'ai';
import { z } from 'zod';

export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { text } = await req.json();

  const { object } = await generateObject({
    model: openai('gpt-4o-mini'),
    schema: z.object({
      sentiment: z.enum(['positive', 'negative', 'neutral']),
      keyTopics: z.array(z.string()),
      summary: z.string().max(200),
      actionItems: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
    }),
    prompt: `Analyze the following text: ${text}`,
  });

  return Response.json(object);
}
Integrating RAG with Vercel AI SDK
Combine the RAG system from Module 3 with the AI SDK to create a codebase-aware assistant.

// app/api/chat/route.ts (with RAG)
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { streamText, tool } from 'ai';
import { z } from 'zod';
import { CodebaseRAG } from '@/lib/rag';

const rag = new CodebaseRAG(process.env.REPO_PATH!);

export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { messages } = await req.json();

  const result = streamText({
    model: openai('gpt-4o'),
    system: `You are an expert on this codebase. Use the query_codebase tool to retrieve relevant code before answering.`,
    messages,
    tools: {
      query_codebase: tool({
        description: 'Search the codebase for relevant functions, classes, or documentation',
        parameters: z.object({
          query: z.string().describe('What information to look for'),
        }),
        execute: async ({ query }) => {
          const results = rag.ask(query, 3);
          return results;
        },
      }),
    },
  });

  return result.toDataStreamResponse();
}
Multi-Provider Support
The AI SDK supports multiple providers. You can even route to different models based on task complexity.

import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { anthropic } from '@ai-sdk/anthropic';
import { google } from '@ai-sdk/google';

// Use different models for different purposes
const fastModel = openai('gpt-4o-mini');
const smartModel = anthropic('claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620');
const longContextModel = google('gemini-1.5-pro');
Building an Agent with maxSteps and onStepFinish
The AI SDK can run multi-step agentic workflows where the model calls tools, processes results, and continues reasoning.

// app/api/agent/route.ts
import { openai } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { streamText } from 'ai';

export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { messages } = await req.json();

  const result = streamText({
    model: openai('gpt-4o'),
    messages,
    tools: { /* ... */ },
    maxSteps: 10, // Allow up to 10 tool call rounds
    onStepFinish: (step) => {
      console.log(`Step ${step.stepNumber} completed`);
      console.log(`Tool calls:`, step.toolCalls);
      console.log(`Tool results:`, step.toolResults);
    },
  });

  return result.toDataStreamResponse();
}
Deploying to Vercel
Vercel is the natural hosting platform for Next.js apps with AI features.

# Install Vercel CLI
npm i -g vercel

# Deploy
vercel

# Add environment variables in Vercel Dashboard
# OPENAI_API_KEY, etc.
Consider these production optimizations:
<ul>
• Use Edge Runtime for lower latency (set `runtime: 'edge'` in route).
• Implement rate limiting with Upstash Redis.
• Add authentication (NextAuth.js or Clerk) to protect API routes.
• Monitor usage with Vercel Analytics and OpenAI dashboard.
Real-World Case Study: Vercel's v0.dev
Vercel's own AI product, v0.dev, is built on the AI SDK. It generates complete React components from natural language descriptions. Key learnings:
<ul>
• They use a combination of fine-tuned models and extensive tool definitions.
• Streaming is essential for perceived performance—users see code appearing in real-time.
• They implemented a sophisticated RAG system to ground generations in their component library.
References and Further Reading
<ul>
• Vercel AI SDK Documentation: Official docs with guides and API reference.
• Next.js App Router: Building applications with the App Router.
• Zod Documentation: Schema validation for TypeScript.
• Vercel AI SDK Examples: GitHub repository with numerous examples.
• OpenAI API Reference: Official API docs.
• Anthropic API: Claude API documentation.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Streaming dramatically improves user experience for AI responses—use it by default.
• The Vercel AI SDK simplifies tool calling and state management, reducing boilerplate by 70-80%.
• Use `generateObject` for non-chat structured outputs like classification, extraction, or form generation.
• Integrate RAG tools to ground AI responses in your own data and codebase.
• Multi-step agentic workflows are possible with `maxSteps` and tool definitions.
• Deploy to Vercel for optimal performance, but the SDK works anywhere Node.js runs.
• The full-stack app you've built here serves as the foundation for incorporating the multi-agent crew from Module 4 into a production web interface.

modules.title: Module 7: Deploying and Scaling AI Agents
content: Moving agents from local scripts to production requires robust infrastructure. This module covers serverless deployment, container orchestration, queue management for long-running tasks, cost optimization strategies, and observability. You'll learn to deploy the multi-agent systems built in previous modules to cloud platforms, ensuring they scale reliably, respond within acceptable latency, and operate cost-effectively.
<div style='background-color: #e8f4fd; border-left: 6px solid #2196F3; padding: 15px; margin: 20px 0;'>
📌 COURSE CONTEXT

This module operationalizes the agents and applications from Modules 1-6. By the end, you'll be able to deploy production-ready AI agent systems that can handle real-world workloads, monitor their performance, and optimize costs. The skills here are essential for turning prototypes into reliable, scalable services.
The Production Gap: Why Local Scripts Aren't Enough
Local agent scripts face fundamental limitations in production environments:
<ul>
• Execution Time Limits: Serverless functions typically cap at 15 minutes (AWS Lambda) or 60 seconds (Vercel Edge). Complex agent tasks may take longer.
• Concurrency and Scaling: Hundreds of simultaneous users require automatic scaling, not a single-threaded script.
• Cost Control: LLM API costs can spiral without proper budgeting and monitoring.
• Reliability: Network timeouts, API rate limits, and model errors must be handled gracefully.
• Observability: You need visibility into agent decisions, tool calls, and performance metrics.
• Security: API keys, user data, and agent actions must be secured and audited.
According to a 2025 survey by LangChain, 67% of teams building AI agents report deployment and scaling as their biggest challenge, surpassing even model selection and prompt engineering.
Deployment Architecture Patterns
1. Synchronous API (Simple Agent)
Best for agents that complete within 30 seconds. Deploy as a REST API endpoint using serverless functions or containers.
User Request → API Gateway → Lambda/Container → Agent Execution → JSON Response
2. Asynchronous Queue-Based (Complex Agent)
For agents that may run for minutes or hours. The API immediately returns a task ID, and the client polls or receives a webhook when complete.
User Request → API → SQS/BullMQ → Worker → Results Storage → Webhook/Email → User
3. Streaming Agent
For real-time interaction (like chat). Uses WebSockets or Server-Sent Events (SSE) to stream agent thoughts and actions as they happen.
Serverless Deployment on AWS Lambda
Lambda is cost-effective for sporadic workloads and scales automatically. However, you must handle the 15-minute timeout and cold starts.
Step 1: Package Your Agent
Create a deployment package with all dependencies. Use Lambda Layers for large dependencies like `crewai`, `langchain`, or ML libraries.

# Directory structure
agent-lambda/
├── lambda_function.py
├── my_agent/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── crew.py
│   └── tools.py
├── requirements.txt
└── .env (excluded, use Lambda environment variables)

# lambda_function.py
import json
import os
from my_agent.crew import run_dev_crew
import boto3
from datetime import datetime

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Lambda entry point for agent execution."""
    try:
        body = json.loads(event.get('body', '{}'))
        user_query = body.get('query', '')
        
        if not user_query:
            return {
                'statusCode': 400,
                'body': json.dumps({'error': 'Missing query parameter'})
            }
        
        # Run the agent (ensure it completes within timeout)
        result = run_dev_crew(user_query)
        
        # Optionally save result to S3 for long outputs
        if len(result) > 256 * 1024:  # Lambda response limit ~256KB
            s3 = boto3.client('s3')
            key = f"results/{context.aws_request_id}.json"
            s3.put_object(
                Bucket=os.environ['RESULTS_BUCKET'],
                Key=key,
                Body=json.dumps(result)
            )
            result = {'s3_reference': key}
        
        return {
            'statusCode': 200,
            'body': json.dumps({'response': result})
        }
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error: {str(e)}")
        return {
            'statusCode': 500,
            'body': json.dumps({'error': str(e)})
        }
Step 2: Create Lambda Layer for Dependencies

# Create a layer for Python dependencies
mkdir -p layer/python
pip install crewai langchain-openai chromadb -t layer/python/
cd layer && zip -r ../agent-layer.zip python/

# Upload layer via AWS Console or CLI
aws lambda publish-layer-version \
    --layer-name agent-dependencies \
    --zip-file fileb://agent-layer.zip \
    --compatible-runtimes python3.11
Step 3: Configure Lambda
<ul>
• Runtime: Python 3.11+
• Memory: 1024-3008 MB (more memory = faster CPU, beneficial for LLM orchestration)
• Timeout: 15 minutes (maximum)
• Environment Variables: OPENAI_API_KEY, RESULTS_BUCKET, etc.
• VPC: Only needed if accessing private resources (RDS, internal APIs)
Step 4: API Gateway Integration
Create a REST API or HTTP API to trigger the Lambda. Consider using WebSocket API for streaming responses.

# API Gateway Configuration (AWS CDK / Terraform recommended)
# POST /agent → Lambda Function
# Optional: Add API key authentication
Managing Long-Running Tasks with Queues
When agent tasks exceed 15 minutes (Lambda limit) or you need better visibility and retry logic, use a queue-based architecture.
Architecture: AWS SQS + Lambda / ECS
Client → API Gateway → Lambda (producer) → SQS Queue → Lambda/ECS (consumer) → DynamoDB (status) → Webhook

# producer_lambda.py - Quickly accepts requests and queues them
import json
import boto3
import uuid

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')
QUEUE_URL = os.environ['AGENT_QUEUE_URL']

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    body = json.loads(event['body'])
    task_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    
    message = {
        'task_id': task_id,
        'query': body['query'],
        'user_id': body.get('user_id', 'anonymous'),
        'webhook_url': body.get('webhook_url')
    }
    
    sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MessageBody=json.dumps(message)
    )
    
    return {
        'statusCode': 202,
        'body': json.dumps({'task_id': task_id, 'status': 'queued'})
    }

# consumer_worker.py - Runs on EC2, ECS, or AWS Batch
import json
import boto3
import requests
from my_agent.crew import run_dev_crew

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table('AgentTasks')

def process_message(message):
    task_id = message['task_id']
    
    # Update status to processing
    table.update_item(
        Key={'task_id': task_id},
        UpdateExpression='SET #status = :status, started_at = :time',
        ExpressionAttributeNames={'#status': 'status'},
        ExpressionAttributeValues={':status': 'processing', ':time': datetime.now().isoformat()}
    )
    
    try:
        result = run_dev_crew(message['query'])
        
        table.update_item(
            Key={'task_id': task_id},
            UpdateExpression='SET #status = :status, result = :result, completed_at = :time',
            ExpressionAttributeNames={'#status': 'status'},
            ExpressionAttributeValues={
                ':status': 'completed',
                ':result': result,
                ':time': datetime.now().isoformat()
            }
        )
        
        # Send webhook if provided
        if message.get('webhook_url'):
            requests.post(message['webhook_url'], json={'task_id': task_id, 'result': result})
            
    except Exception as e:
        table.update_item(
            Key={'task_id': task_id},
            UpdateExpression='SET #status = :status, error = :error',
            ExpressionAttributeNames={'#status': 'status'},
            ExpressionAttributeValues={':status': 'failed', ':error': str(e)}
        )

# Poll SQS continuously
while True:
    response = sqs.receive_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MaxNumberOfMessages=1,
        WaitTimeSeconds=20
    )
    for msg in response.get('Messages', []):
        process_message(json.loads(msg['Body']))
        sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, ReceiptHandle=msg['ReceiptHandle'])
Containerization with Docker and Kubernetes
For complete control, package agents as Docker containers and orchestrate with Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS) or ECS.

# Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    gcc \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python packages
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Run the consumer worker
CMD ["python", "consumer_worker.py"]

# Kubernetes Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: agent-worker
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: agent-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: agent-worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: your-registry/agent-worker:latest
        env:
        - name: OPENAI_API_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: agent-secrets
              key: openai-key
        - name: AGENT_QUEUE_URL
          value: "https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/agent-queue"
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
Cost Optimization Strategies
LLM API costs can quickly dominate your budget. Implement these strategies:
1. Model Tiering
<ul>
• Use GPT-4o / Claude 3.5 Sonnet only for complex reasoning (architect, reviewer).
• Use GPT-4o-mini / Claude Haiku for simpler tasks (test generation, summarization).
• Use local models (Ollama with Llama 3.1 8B) for non-critical agents.

# Model router based on task complexity
def get_model_for_task(task_description: str):
    complexity_keywords = ['design', 'architecture', 'security', 'review', 'refactor']
    if any(kw in task_description.lower() for kw in complexity_keywords):
        return openai('gpt-4o')
    else:
        return openai('gpt-4o-mini')
2. Prompt Caching
Anthropic's Claude offers prompt caching, reducing costs by up to 90% for repeated system prompts and context. Structure your agent prompts to maximize cache hits.
3. Token Budgeting and Rate Limiting

import tiktoken

def estimate_cost(messages, model="gpt-4o"):
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    total_tokens = sum(len(enc.encode(msg['content'])) for msg in messages)
    # Approximate pricing (check current rates)
    input_cost = (total_tokens / 1_000_000) * 2.50  # $2.50 per 1M tokens
    output_cost_estimate = (500 / 1_000_000) * 10.00  # Assume 500 output tokens
    return input_cost + output_cost_estimate

# Enforce budget per user per day
def check_budget(user_id):
    usage = get_daily_usage(user_id)
    if usage > DAILY_BUDGET:
        raise Exception("Daily token budget exceeded")
4. Caching LLM Responses
Cache frequent queries using semantic similarity or exact match.

from redis import Redis
import hashlib

redis_client = Redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'])

def cached_llm_call(prompt, model, ttl=3600):
    cache_key = hashlib.md5(f"{model}:{prompt}".encode()).hexdigest()
    cached = redis_client.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return cached.decode()
    
    response = call_llm(prompt, model)
    redis_client.setex(cache_key, ttl, response)
    return response
Observability and Monitoring
You can't improve what you don't measure. Instrument your agents with structured logging and metrics.
Key Metrics to Track
<ul>
• Latency: p50, p95, p99 of agent execution time.
• Token Usage: Input and output tokens per request, aggregated.
• Cost: Estimated USD per request and cumulative.
• Success Rate: Percentage of tasks that complete without error.
• Tool Call Distribution: Which tools are most used, and their failure rates.

# Using OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter

trace.set_tracer_provider(TracerProvider())
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def run_agent_with_tracing(query):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_execution") as span:
        span.set_attribute("query", query)
        span.set_attribute("agent.type", "dev_crew")
        
        result = run_dev_crew(query)
        
        span.set_attribute("result_length", len(str(result)))
        return result
Send traces and metrics to Datadog, New Relic, or open-source SigNoz.
Real-World Case Study: Deploying AI Agents at Scale
Company: A tech startup building an AI-powered code review service. They use CrewAI agents to analyze pull requests and suggest improvements.
Scale: 10,000+ PRs analyzed per month.
Architecture:
<ul>
• GitHub webhook → API Gateway → SQS → ECS Fargate workers.
• Workers run in Docker containers with CrewAI and custom tools.
• Results stored in DynamoDB and posted back as GitHub comments.
• Prometheus + Grafana for monitoring; alerts on high error rates.
Key Optimizations:
<ul>
• Caching of file embeddings to avoid re-indexing unchanged files.
• Using GPT-4o-mini for 80% of PRs, escalating to GPT-4o only for complex changes.
• Implementing a token budget per repository owner.
Cost: ~$0.15 per PR analysis, well within their business model.
Security Considerations for Deployed Agents
<ul>
• Secrets Management: Never hardcode API keys. Use AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or environment variables from a secure source.
• Network Isolation: Run agents in private subnets; use VPC endpoints for AWS services.
• Input Validation: Sanitize user inputs to prevent prompt injection attacks.
• Output Sanitization: Ensure agent outputs don't leak sensitive data (PII, secrets).
• Audit Logging: Log every agent action and tool call for compliance and forensics.
• IAM Least Privilege: Lambda/ECS roles should only have permissions necessary for their tasks.
References and Further Reading
<ul>
• AWS Lambda Developer Guide: Official documentation.
• AWS SQS: Managed message queues.
• OpenTelemetry: Observability framework for traces and metrics.
• Vercel AI SDK Deployment: Production best practices.
• LangChain Deployment Guide: Options for deploying LangChain apps.
• Terraform AWS Modules: Infrastructure as code for Lambda.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Production agents need async patterns—use queues (SQS, BullMQ) for tasks exceeding function timeouts.
• Serverless (Lambda) is great for sporadic, short-running agents; containers (ECS/K8s) provide more control for long-running or GPU workloads.
• Cost optimization is critical: model tiering, prompt caching, and response caching can reduce LLM costs by 50-80%.
• Implement comprehensive observability—logs, metrics, and traces—to debug and improve agent performance.
• Security is non-negotiable: manage secrets properly, validate inputs, and follow least privilege principles.
• The deployment patterns learned here apply to any agent framework (CrewAI, LangChain, custom).
• With these skills, you can take the agents from previous modules and make them available to real users at scale.

modules.title: Module 8: Securing AI-Generated Code
content: AI can inadvertently generate vulnerable code—SQL injection, hardcoded secrets, insecure deserialization, and more. This module teaches a defense-in-depth approach to securing AI-generated code: secure prompting techniques, automated static analysis in CI/CD, and runtime protection. You'll learn to integrate security into every stage of the AI-assisted development lifecycle, ensuring that the productivity gains from AI don't come at the cost of security.
<div style='background-color: #e8f4fd; border-left: 6px solid #2196F3; padding: 15px; margin: 20px 0;'>
📌 COURSE CONTEXT

This module applies the prompting expertise from Module 2 to the security domain and integrates with the CI/CD pipelines from Module 7. By the end, you'll be able to confidently use AI coding assistants while maintaining—or even improving—your application's security posture.
The Security Risks of AI-Generated Code

LLMs are trained on vast public code repositories, including insecure examples. Without explicit security guidance, they reproduce common vulnerabilities. A 2024 Stanford study found that developers using AI assistants wrote significantly less secure code—those with access to AI assistants were more likely to introduce security bugs than those coding manually, especially when they trusted the AI's output.
Specific risks include:
<ul>
• Injection Flaws: SQL, NoSQL, OS command injection from string concatenation.
• Hardcoded Secrets: API keys, passwords, tokens embedded in code.
• Insecure Deserialization: Using `pickle.loads` on untrusted data.
• Weak Cryptography: MD5/SHA1 for passwords, hardcoded IVs, ECB mode.
• Path Traversal: Unsanitized user input in file paths.
• Outdated Dependencies: Suggesting vulnerable library versions.
• Missing Authentication/Authorization Checks: Generating endpoints without proper access controls.

According to Snyk's 2025 AI Code Security Report, 56% of organizations have discovered security vulnerabilities in AI-generated code that reached production. The good news: with proper guardrails, AI can actually help write more secure code than the average developer.
Strategy 1: Secure Prompt Engineering
The first line of defense is instructing the AI to prioritize security. Explicit security requirements in prompts reduce vulnerabilities by 40-60%.
Basic Secure Prompt Template

secure_prompt = """
Write a Python function to query a user by ID from a PostgreSQL database.

Security Requirements (CRITICAL):
- Use parameterized queries (psycopg2 placeholders %s) - NEVER string concatenation.
- Validate that the input ID is a positive integer. Return 400 for invalid input.
- Handle database errors gracefully without exposing schema details or stack traces.
- Use environment variables for database credentials, never hardcode them.
- Include logging for security-relevant events (failed queries, invalid inputs).
- Return only necessary user fields, never the password hash.
- Include comments explaining security measures.

Functional Requirements:
- Use async/await with asyncpg or psycopg2.
- Return a User dataclass or dict.

Write the complete, production-ready function.
"""
Security-Aware System Prompt for AI Assistants
If you're using an AI coding assistant (Copilot, Cursor, Codeium), customize the system prompt or project rules.

// .cursorrules or similar configuration
You are an expert secure coder. Follow these rules strictly:
1. Never generate code with hardcoded secrets. Use environment variables.
2. Always use parameterized queries for database access. Never string concatenation.
3. Validate and sanitize all user inputs. Use allowlists, not blocklists.
4. Apply the principle of least privilege.
5. Use secure cryptographic libraries (bcrypt for passwords, cryptography for encryption).
6. Include proper error handling that doesn't leak sensitive information.
7. Generate code with input validation, proper authentication checks, and CSRF protection for web endpoints.
8. When suggesting dependencies, always specify the latest secure version.
9. Flag potential security issues in comments when generating code.
10. For file operations, validate paths and prevent directory traversal.
Few-Shot Prompting for Secure Patterns
Provide examples of secure code alongside insecure counterparts to reinforce the pattern.

prompt = """
Write a function to fetch a product by ID from a database.

Insecure example (DO NOT DO THIS):
```python
def get_product_bad(product_id):
    query = f"SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = {product_id}"
    return db.execute(query)
```

Secure example (FOLLOW THIS PATTERN):
```python
def get_product_good(product_id):
    # Validate input
    if not isinstance(product_id, int) or product_id <= 0:
        raise ValueError("Invalid product ID")
    query = "SELECT * FROM products WHERE id = %s"
    return db.execute(query, (product_id,))
```

Now, write a function to delete a user by email, following the secure pattern.
"""
Strategy 2: Automated Code Scanning in CI/CD
Prompting helps but isn't foolproof. Automated static analysis acts as a safety net, catching vulnerabilities before they merge.
Tool Comparison
<ul>
• Semgrep: Fast, open-source, rule-based static analysis. Excellent for custom rules and CI/CD integration.
• CodeQL (GitHub): Deep semantic analysis, powerful for finding complex vulnerabilities. Free for public repos.
• Snyk Code: AI-powered, developer-friendly, integrates with IDEs.
• SonarQube: Comprehensive code quality and security platform.
• Bandit: Python-specific security linter.
• ESLint with security plugins: For JavaScript/TypeScript.
GitHub Actions Workflow with Multiple Scanners

# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Scanning

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, develop]
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  semgrep:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run Semgrep
        uses: returntocorp/semgrep-action@v1
        with:
          config: >-
            p/owasp-top-ten
            p/secrets
            p/sql-injection
            p/python
            p/javascript
        env:
          SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN }}

  codeql:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      security-events: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Initialize CodeQL
        uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
        with:
          languages: python, javascript
      - name: Perform CodeQL Analysis
        uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3

  bandit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      - name: Install Bandit
        run: pip install bandit
      - name: Run Bandit
        run: bandit -r . -f json -o bandit-report.json || true
      - name: Upload Bandit Report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: bandit-report
          path: bandit-report.json

  gitleaks:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Run Gitleaks
        uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
Custom Semgrep Rules for AI-Generated Code Patterns
Create rules targeting vulnerabilities commonly found in LLM output.

# custom-semgrep-rules.yaml
rules:
  - id: insecure-sql-concatenation
    pattern: |
      cursor.execute("SELECT ... " + $VAR + ...)
    message: "SQL query built with string concatenation. Use parameterized queries."
    languages: [python]
    severity: ERROR

  - id: hardcoded-openai-key
    pattern: |
      openai.api_key = "..."
    message: "Hardcoded OpenAI API key detected. Use environment variables."
    languages: [python]
    severity: ERROR

  - id: insecure-pickle-load
    pattern: pickle.loads($DATA)
    message: "Unsafe pickle deserialization. Use JSON or validate input source."
    languages: [python]
    severity: ERROR

  - id: weak-password-hash
    pattern-either:
      - pattern: hashlib.md5($PASSWORD)
      - pattern: hashlib.sha1($PASSWORD)
    message: "Weak password hashing detected. Use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2."
    languages: [python]
    severity: WARNING
Strategy 3: AI-Assisted Security Review
Use AI to help review AI-generated code. A second LLM (or the same with a different prompt) can catch vulnerabilities the first missed.
Dual LLM Review Pattern

def secure_code_generation(prompt):
    # Step 1: Generate code with security-focused prompt
    code = generate_code(secure_prompt(prompt))
    
    # Step 2: Have a "security reviewer" LLM analyze it
    review_prompt = f"""
    You are a security expert. Review this code for vulnerabilities.
    Check for: SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded secrets, insecure crypto, path traversal.
    If you find issues, provide the fixed code. If clean, respond "SECURITY_OK".
    
    Code:
    ```
    {code}
    ```
    """
    review = generate_code(review_prompt)
    
    if "SECURITY_OK" in review:
        return code
    else:
        return extract_fixed_code(review)
Strategy 4: Runtime Protection and Monitoring
Even with best efforts, some vulnerabilities slip through. Runtime protections provide a final safety net.
<ul>
• Web Application Firewall (WAF): Blocks SQL injection, XSS, and other attacks at the edge.
• Runtime Application Self-Protection (RASP): Instrumentation that detects and blocks attacks from within the application.
• Secrets Detection in Logs: Tools like TruffleHog or GitGuardian scanning logs for leaked credentials.
• Dependency Scanning: Regularly run `npm audit`, `pip-audit`, or Snyk to catch vulnerable libraries.
Real-World Case Study: AI-Generated Vulnerability at a Fintech

A fintech startup used an AI assistant to generate an API endpoint for user profile updates. The generated code looked correct but contained a critical IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerability—it didn't verify that the authenticated user owned the profile being updated. The code passed code review because it "looked clean."

The vulnerability was discovered in a penetration test. Fortunately, it was fixed before exploitation. Post-mortem analysis showed the AI had generated the endpoint based on patterns from tutorials that omitted authorization checks for simplicity.
Lessons Learned:
<ul>
• Always include explicit authorization requirements in prompts.
• Automated scanning (Semgrep rules for missing auth checks) would have caught this.
• Never trust AI-generated code without thorough security review.
Building a Security Gate in CI/CD for AI-Generated Code
For teams heavily using AI code generation, consider a dedicated security gate that runs additional checks on files with high AI contribution.

# .github/workflows/ai-code-security-gate.yml
name: AI Code Security Gate
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  check-ai-files:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Identify AI-generated files
        id: ai-files
        run: |
          # Files with AI attribution comment or from specific paths
          AI_FILES=$(grep -rl "Generated by AI" --include="*.py" --include="*.js" . || true)
          echo "files=$AI_FILES" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      - name: Enhanced security scan for AI files
        if: steps.ai-files.outputs.files != ''
        run: |
          # Run more aggressive security checks
          semgrep --config=auto --config=p/owasp-top-ten --config=p/secrets ${{ steps.ai-files.outputs.files }} --error
      - name: Require human review for AI files
        if: steps.ai-files.outputs.files != ''
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: '⚠️ This PR contains AI-generated code. A security-focused review is required before merging.'
            })
References and Further Reading
<ul>
• OWASP Top 10 (2025): The definitive list of web application security risks.
• OWASP AI Security and Privacy Guide: Guidance specific to AI systems.
• Stanford Study on AI and Security: Do Users Write More Insecure Code with AI Assistants? (Perry et al., 2022).
• Semgrep Documentation: Writing custom rules and CI integration.
• GitHub CodeQL: Semantic code analysis.
• Snyk AI Code Security: AI-powered SAST.
• Gitleaks: Secrets detection.
• CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses: MITRE's annual list.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Never trust AI-generated code blindly—treat it as untrusted input requiring validation.
• Secure prompting reduces vulnerabilities by 40-60%; always include explicit security requirements.
• Automated scanning (Semgrep, CodeQL, Bandit) in CI/CD acts as a critical safety net.
• Use a dual-LLM review pattern: one to generate, another to security-review.
• Implement custom Semgrep rules targeting common AI-generated vulnerability patterns.
• Runtime protections (WAF, RASP) provide defense-in-depth against missed vulnerabilities.
• For teams using AI extensively, consider a dedicated security gate for AI-generated files.
• Security is a continuous process—regularly update prompts, rules, and scanning tools as new threats emerge.

modules.title: Module 9: Project Prompts & Blueprints for AI-Driven Development
content: This module provides curated prompts, architectural blueprints, and multi-agent workflows for building real-world projects using AI agents. These are battle-tested templates you can adapt for hackathons, startup MVPs, or internal tools. Use these as starting points for your own work, customizing the tech stack, features, and agent roles to fit your needs.
<div style='background-color: #e8f4fd; border-left: 6px solid #2196F3; padding: 15px; margin: 20px 0;'>
📌 COURSE CONTEXT

This module synthesizes everything from the course: agent design (Module 1), prompt engineering (Module 2), RAG (Module 3), multi-agent systems (Module 4), testing (Module 5), full-stack development (Module 6), deployment (Module 7), and security (Module 8). The blueprints here are ready to feed into your CrewAI or custom agent systems.
Why Project Blueprints Matter
Starting an AI-driven project from scratch is overwhelming. The right prompt can generate an entire scaffold, but only if it's structured correctly. Well-crafted project prompts:
<ul>
• Save 10-20 hours of initial architecture and boilerplate work.
• Ensure consistency with best practices (folder structure, naming conventions).
• Provide a clear roadmap for multi-agent teams to execute.
• Reduce decision fatigue by pre-selecting proven tech stacks.
Blueprint Structure
Each blueprint includes:
<ul>
• Project Overview: Goal, features, and target users.
• Recommended Tech Stack: Justified choices for frontend, backend, database, and AI integration.
• Agent Team Composition: Which agents (from a CrewAI-style setup) should handle which parts.
• Detailed Prompts per Agent: Copy-paste-ready prompts for each phase.
• Expected Outputs: What files or artifacts each agent should produce.
• Testing & Deployment Checklist: Steps to production readiness.
Project 1: AI-Powered E-Commerce Backend
Project Overview
Goal: Build a production-ready REST API for an e-commerce platform with authentication, product catalog, shopping cart, and order processing.
Key Features:
<ul>
• User registration/login (JWT authentication).
• Role-based access control (customer, admin).
• Product management (CRUD for admins, list/search for customers).
• Shopping cart with session or user association.
• Order creation, payment simulation, and order history.
• Inventory management (stock decrement on order).
• API documentation (OpenAPI/Swagger).
Recommended Tech Stack:
<ul>
• Backend: Node.js + Express (TypeScript) or Python + FastAPI.
• Database: PostgreSQL (primary), Redis (caching/session).
• ORM: Prisma (Node) or SQLAlchemy (Python).
• Authentication: JWT with refresh tokens.
• Validation: Zod (Node) or Pydantic (Python).
• Container: Docker + docker-compose.
Agent Team Composition
<ul>
• Product Manager Agent: Refines requirements into user stories and acceptance criteria.
• Architect Agent: Designs database schema, API endpoints, and system architecture.
• Backend Agent: Implements the API routes, middleware, and business logic.
• Security Reviewer Agent: Audits code for OWASP vulnerabilities and suggests fixes.
• DevOps Agent: Creates Dockerfile, docker-compose, and deployment configs.
Prompt for Product Manager Agent

You are a Senior Product Manager for an e-commerce platform.

Create a detailed product requirements document (PRD) for a REST API backend with the following high-level features:
- User authentication and authorization (JWT, roles: customer, admin)
- Product catalog (CRUD for admin, list/search for all)
- Shopping cart (add/remove items, persist across sessions for logged-in users)
- Order placement and order history
- Basic inventory management (stock decrement on successful order)

For each feature, provide:
1. User stories (As a... I want... So that...)
2. Acceptance criteria (bullet points, testable)
3. Edge cases and error scenarios
4. Non-functional requirements (performance, security, scalability)

Format as a markdown document with clear sections.
Prompt for Architect Agent

You are a Principal Solutions Architect. Based on the following PRD, design the technical architecture.

[PASTE PRD FROM PRODUCT MANAGER HERE]

Provide:
1. PostgreSQL database schema (DDL statements with CREATE TABLE). Include:
   - Users (id, email, password_hash, role, created_at, updated_at)
   - Products (id, name, description, price, stock_quantity, category, created_at)
   - Carts (id, user_id, created_at, updated_at)
   - CartItems (id, cart_id, product_id, quantity)
   - Orders (id, user_id, total_amount, status, shipping_address, created_at)
   - OrderItems (id, order_id, product_id, quantity, price_at_time)
   Add appropriate indexes (email lookup, product search, foreign keys).

2. REST API endpoint design (list all endpoints with HTTP methods, paths, request/response schemas).
   Include authentication requirements for each.

3. High-level architecture diagram described in text (components: API Gateway, App Servers, PostgreSQL, Redis).

4. Technology stack recommendations with rationale.

5. Security considerations (password hashing algorithm, rate limiting, input validation strategy).

Output as a detailed markdown document.
Prompt for Backend Agent (Node.js/Express/TypeScript)

You are a Senior Backend Engineer specializing in Node.js, Express, TypeScript, and Prisma.

Implement the e-commerce API based on the following architecture document:

[PASTE ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT HERE]

Requirements:
- Use Express with TypeScript. Organize code with a clean architecture: routes, controllers, services, repositories.
- Use Prisma ORM with PostgreSQL. Generate the Prisma schema from the DDL provided.
- Implement JWT authentication middleware (verify token, attach user to request).
- Implement role-based authorization (admin-only routes).
- Use Zod for request validation.
- Include comprehensive error handling (custom error classes, global error handler).
- Add logging with Winston or Pino.
- Write unit tests for critical paths (auth, order creation) using Jest.
- Generate OpenAPI/Swagger documentation (use tsoa or swagger-jsdoc).

Output all code files with proper directory structure. Include a README with setup instructions.
Prompt for Security Reviewer Agent

You are a Staff Security Engineer. Conduct a thorough security review of the e-commerce backend code.

[PASTE BACKEND CODE FILES OR SUMMARY]

Check for:
1. OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, broken access control, security misconfiguration).
2. Authentication flaws (weak JWT secret, missing refresh token rotation).
3. Authorization bypasses (missing checks on user-owned resources).
4. Input validation gaps (relying solely on Zod? Check edge cases).
5. Sensitive data exposure (passwords, emails in logs).
6. Rate limiting and brute force protection.
7. Dependency vulnerabilities (run npm audit equivalent).

Provide a report with:
- Critical issues (must fix)
- High, Medium, Low severity findings
- Specific file and line numbers
- Remediation code snippets
- Overall security score (1-10)

Output as a markdown report.
Prompt for DevOps Agent

You are a DevOps Engineer. Containerize the e-commerce backend for production deployment.

Based on the Node.js/TypeScript codebase:
1. Write a multi-stage Dockerfile (build stage, production stage). Optimize for image size.
2. Write a docker-compose.yml that includes:
   - The Node.js app service
   - PostgreSQL service (with volume for persistence)
   - Redis service (optional, for rate limiting)
3. Provide a .env.example file with all required environment variables.
4. Write a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) that:
   - Runs tests and security scans (Semgrep, npm audit)
   - Builds and pushes Docker image to GitHub Container Registry (or Docker Hub)
   - (Optional) Deploys to a cloud service (Railway, Render, or AWS ECS).

Output all files with clear comments.
Project 2: AI Content Moderation Dashboard
Project Overview
Goal: Build a Next.js application where users can submit comments, and an AI agent moderates them for toxicity, spam, and custom policy violations in real-time.
Key Features:
<ul>
• User-submitted comments with real-time moderation feedback.
• Moderation dashboard for admins to review flagged content.
• Configurable moderation policies (toxicity threshold, banned keywords).
• Analytics on moderation trends (comments flagged per day, top violation types).
• Optional: User appeals process for false positives.
Recommended Tech Stack:
<ul>
• Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui.
• Backend API: Next.js API routes (or separate Express/FastAPI).
• Database: PostgreSQL (via Prisma or Drizzle) or Supabase.
• AI Moderation: OpenAI Moderation API, Perspective API, or a fine-tuned local model.
• Real-time: Vercel AI SDK streaming or WebSockets (Pusher/Socket.io).
Prompt for Full-Stack Agent (Next.js)

You are a Senior Full-Stack Developer expert in Next.js 14, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.

Create a complete content moderation application with the following features:

1. Homepage (`/`):
   - A textarea for users to submit comments.
   - On submission, the comment is sent to `/api/moderate`.
   - Display the submitted comment below with a colored badge: Green (Safe, score < 0.3), Yellow (Review, 0.3-0.7), Red (Flagged, >0.7).
   - Show a loading state while moderation is in progress.

2. API Route `/api/moderate`:
   - Accepts POST with JSON `{ text: string }`.
   - Calls OpenAI Moderation API (or Perspective API). Parse the response.
   - Returns JSON with `{ score: number, categories: object, flagged: boolean }`.
   - Store the comment and its moderation result in a PostgreSQL database (via Prisma).

3. Admin Dashboard (`/admin`):
   - Protected by a simple password (environment variable) or NextAuth.
   - Displays a table of all comments with: text snippet, score, status, timestamp.
   - Ability to filter by status (flagged, safe, pending review).
   - Ability to override the AI decision (mark as safe/unsafe) which updates the database.

4. Database Schema (Prisma):
   model Comment {
     id        String   @id @default(cuid())
     text      String
     score     Float
     categories Json?   // Store moderation categories
     status    String   @default("pending") // pending, approved, rejected
     createdAt DateTime @default(now())
     updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
   }

5. Styling: Use Tailwind CSS with shadcn/ui components (Button, Textarea, Table, Badge).

6. Environment Variables: OPENAI_API_KEY, ADMIN_PASSWORD, DATABASE_URL.

Provide all code files with proper directory structure (app/, components/, lib/, prisma/).
Include a README with setup and deployment instructions.
Project 3: AI-Powered Internal Knowledge Base Q&A (RAG)
Goal: Build a Slack bot or web app that answers employee questions based on internal documentation (Notion, Google Drive, Markdown files) using RAG.
Agent Team: Indexing Agent (processes docs), Retrieval Agent (finds relevant chunks), Q&A Agent (generates answers).

You are an AI Engineer building a RAG system for internal documentation.

Requirements:
1. Data Ingestion: Script to recursively crawl a directory of Markdown files (or fetch from Notion API).
2. Chunking: Split documents into ~500 token chunks with 50 token overlap.
3. Embedding: Use OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` or a local model.
4. Vector Store: Use ChromaDB (local) or Pinecone (cloud).
5. Query Endpoint: FastAPI endpoint `/ask` that accepts a question, retrieves top 5 chunks, and uses GPT-4o to generate an answer with citations.
6. Slack Integration: Slack slash command `/ask [question]` that calls the endpoint and returns the answer.
7. Feedback Loop: Buttons for "Helpful" / "Not Helpful" that log to a database for future fine-tuning.

Provide complete implementation with clear separation of concerns.
Project 4: AI Code Review Bot (GitHub Integration)
Goal: A GitHub App that automatically reviews pull requests, suggests improvements, and flags security issues.

You are building a GitHub App for AI-powered code review.

Features:
- Triggered on `pull_request` events (opened, synchronize).
- Fetches the diff and relevant context (files changed).
- Sends code to an LLM with a security-focused review prompt.
- Posts review comments inline on the PR (via GitHub API).
- Categorizes findings: Security, Performance, Style, Bug.
- Configurable via `.github/ai-review.yml` (ignore patterns, custom rules).

Implementation:
- Probot or GitHub Actions (JavaScript/TypeScript).
- Use OpenAI or Claude API.
- Deploy as a serverless function (Vercel, AWS Lambda).

Provide the complete Probot app code, review prompt template, and deployment instructions.
Blueprint Customization Guide
Adapt these blueprints to your specific needs:
<ul>
• Change the Tech Stack: Replace "Node.js/Express" with "Python/FastAPI" by updating the prompt. Specify "Use SQLAlchemy instead of Prisma."
• Add Features: Append to the feature list in the Product Manager prompt. Example: "Add wishlist functionality and product reviews."
• Change Agent Roles: If you prefer a single agent for a simpler project, collapse roles. If more complex, add specialized agents (e.g., "Database Optimization Agent").
• Use Different AI Providers: Specify "Use Claude 3.5 Sonnet via Anthropic API" in the prompt.
• Local Models: Replace "OpenAI" with "Ollama with Llama 3.1 8B" and adjust prompt expectations accordingly.
Real-World Case Study: Building an MVP in 3 Days with Blueprints
A solo developer used the e-commerce blueprint (Project 1) to build a functional MVP for a niche marketplace in three days. Process:
<ul>
• Day 1: Ran Product Manager and Architect prompts with Claude. Reviewed and refined the PRD and schema.
• Day 2: Used the Backend Agent prompt in Cursor. Generated 80% of the API code. Manually fixed a few edge cases and added payment integration (Stripe).
• Day 3: Ran DevOps and Security prompts. Containerized the app, set up CI/CD, and deployed to Railway.
The MVP successfully handled 100 beta users and raised a small pre-seed round based on the demo. The developer estimated the blueprints saved at least 60 hours of work.
Best Practices for Using Project Blueprints
<ul>

• Iterate, Don't Expect Perfection: The first output may need refinement. Use follow-up prompts like "Add pagination to the product list endpoint" or "Refactor the authentication to use HTTP-only cookies."
• Review Critically: Treat AI output as a junior developer's pull request. Review for correctness, security, and alignment with your specific business logic.
• Version Control Everything: Commit after each major generation step. This allows rollback if the AI goes off-track.
• Use the Blueprints as Training: Reading the generated code and architecture helps you learn patterns faster than starting from scratch.
• Combine with RAG (Module 3): If you have existing codebase patterns, index them and reference them in prompts: "Follow the existing repository structure in [link to indexed repo]."
References and Further Reading
<ul>
• Awesome ChatGPT Prompts for Developers: Curated list of prompts for coding tasks.
• OpenAI Cookbook: Examples of structured prompts for various use cases.
• Anthropic Prompt Library: Production-ready prompts for Claude.
• V0.dev Prompts: Vercel's AI UI generator—study how they structure prompts for component generation.
• Indie Hackers AI Prompts: Community-shared prompts for building SaaS MVPs.
Key Takeaways
<ul>
• Well-structured, multi-agent prompts can generate entire project scaffolds—from PRD to deployment configs.
• Break large projects into domain-specific prompts for each agent role (PM, Architect, Developer, DevOps, Security).
• Blueprints save 10-60+ hours of initial work and ensure consistency with best practices.
• Customize blueprints by swapping tech stacks, adding features, or changing AI providers in the prompts.
• Always review AI-generated code critically—use it as a powerful starting point, not a finished product.
• Combine these blueprints with the RAG and multi-agent systems from earlier modules for even more powerful workflows.
• The projects here are real-world ready; adapt and deploy them to build your portfolio or launch your next startup.

modules.title: Module 10: Capstone – Autonomous AI Dev Team Project
content: You will orchestrate a team of AI agents to build a complete web application from scratch. You act as the 'Lead Developer' overseeing the agents, making strategic decisions, reviewing outputs, and integrating the components into a cohesive, production-ready system. This capstone synthesizes every skill from the Professional AI-Agent Development course: agent design, prompt engineering, RAG, multi-agent orchestration, testing, deployment, and security.
<div style='background-color: #e8f4fd; border-left: 6px solid #2196F3; padding: 15px; margin: 20px 0;'>
📌 CAPSTONE OBJECTIVE

By the end of this module, you will have built a fully functional AI-Powered Personal Finance Tracker using an autonomous AI development team. You will also have a portfolio piece demonstrating your ability to leverage AI agents for end-to-end software development. This project mirrors real-world AI-augmented workflows used by innovative engineering teams.
Project: AI-Powered Personal Finance Tracker
Goal: Build a web application that helps users track income and expenses, categorize transactions automatically using AI, visualize spending patterns, and export reports.
Core Features:
<ul>
• User Authentication: Sign up, login, logout with JWT (or NextAuth).
• Transaction Management: Add, edit, delete transactions (amount, date, description, category).
• AI Category Suggestion: When a user enters a transaction description, an AI model suggests the most likely category (e.g., 'Uber' → 'Transportation').
• Dashboard with Charts: Visualize income vs. expenses, spending by category (pie/bar charts).
• Export Reports: Download transaction history as CSV or PDF.
• Responsive Design: Works on desktop and mobile.
Stretch Goals (Optional):
<ul>
• Budget setting and alerts.
• Bank account linking via Plaid API.
• Recurring transaction detection.
Recommended Tech Stack:
<ul>
• Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Recharts.
• Backend: Next.js API routes (or separate FastAPI).
• Database: PostgreSQL with Prisma ORM.
• Authentication: NextAuth.js (or Clerk, Supabase Auth).
• AI Integration: OpenAI API (GPT-4o-mini) for category suggestion.
• Deployment: Vercel (frontend/backend) + Supabase or Neon for PostgreSQL.
The AI Development Team

We'll assemble a crew of specialized AI agents using CrewAI (or a custom orchestration layer). Each agent has a clear role, backstory, and set of tools. As the Lead Developer, you will define tasks, review outputs, and handle integration.
Agent Roster
<ul>
• Product Agent: Translates the project vision into a detailed PRD with user stories and acceptance criteria.
• Architect Agent: Designs the database schema, API endpoints, and system architecture.
• UI/UX Agent: Generates React component code with Tailwind and shadcn/ui, ensuring responsive design.
• Backend Agent: Implements API routes, Prisma models, and business logic.
• AI Integration Agent: Builds the category suggestion feature using OpenAI function calling or structured outputs.
• Integration Agent: Connects frontend to backend, manages state (React Query, Zustand), and handles data fetching.
• Test Agent: Writes unit, integration, and end-to-end tests using Jest, React Testing Library, and Playwright.
• Documentation Agent: Creates README, API documentation, and user guide.
Step-by-Step Execution Plan
As Lead Developer, you'll guide the agents through sequential phases. Each phase produces artifacts that feed into the next.
Phase 1: Planning & Architecture
Product Agent Prompt:

You are a Senior Product Manager. Create a detailed Product Requirements Document (PRD) for a Personal Finance Tracker web application.

Features required:
- User authentication (sign up, login, logout)
- Transaction management (CRUD: description, amount, date, category)
- AI-powered category suggestion based on transaction description
- Dashboard with spending charts (income vs expense, category breakdown)
- Export reports (CSV)

For each feature, provide:
1. User story (As a... I want... So that...)
2. Acceptance criteria (bullet points)
3. Edge cases (e.g., negative amounts, future dates)

Also include non-functional requirements: performance, security, accessibility.

Format as a markdown document.
Architect Agent Prompt:

You are a Principal Solutions Architect. Based on the PRD below, design the technical architecture for the Personal Finance Tracker.

[PASTE PRD HERE]

Provide:
1. Database schema (PostgreSQL with Prisma). Include models: User, Transaction, Category (predefined or user-customizable).
2. API endpoint design (RESTful or tRPC). List all endpoints with methods, paths, request/response schemas.
3. High-level architecture diagram (described in text): Next.js frontend, API routes, PostgreSQL, OpenAI API integration.
4. Technology stack justification.
5. Security considerations: password hashing, JWT storage, input validation.

Output as a markdown document.
Phase 2: Backend Implementation
Backend Agent Prompt:

You are a Senior Backend Engineer expert in Next.js API routes, TypeScript, and Prisma.

Implement the backend for the Personal Finance Tracker based on the architecture document below:

[PASTE ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT HERE]

Requirements:
- Use Next.js 14 App Router with API routes in `app/api/`.
- Set up Prisma with PostgreSQL. Generate the schema from the provided design.
- Implement authentication using NextAuth.js with credentials provider (email/password).
- Create API endpoints for:
  - POST /api/auth/register, POST /api/auth/login, POST /api/auth/logout
  - GET /api/transactions, POST /api/transactions, PUT /api/transactions/[id], DELETE /api/transactions/[id]
  - GET /api/categories (predefined list)
  - GET /api/analytics/spending-by-category, GET /api/analytics/monthly-summary
- Protect all transaction and analytics endpoints with authentication middleware.
- Use Zod for request validation.
- Include proper error handling and logging.

Provide all code files with proper directory structure. Include a `prisma/schema.prisma` file.
Phase 3: AI Integration
AI Integration Agent Prompt:

You are an AI Engineer. Implement the AI-powered category suggestion feature.

Requirements:
- Create an API endpoint `POST /api/ai/suggest-category` that accepts `{ description: string }`.
- Use OpenAI's GPT-4o-mini (or GPT-3.5) with a prompt that suggests a category from a predefined list: ['Food', 'Transportation', 'Entertainment', 'Utilities', 'Shopping', 'Healthcare', 'Income', 'Other'].
- The prompt should include few-shot examples.
- Return JSON: `{ suggestedCategory: string, confidence: number }`.
- Cache responses in Redis (optional) to reduce API costs.
- Protect the endpoint with authentication.

Provide the implementation code, prompt template, and any necessary utilities.
Example Prompt Template for AI Agent to Generate:

const categoryPrompt = (description: string) => `
You are a personal finance assistant. Given a transaction description, suggest the most appropriate category.

Categories: Food, Transportation, Entertainment, Utilities, Shopping, Healthcare, Income, Other.

Examples:
- "Uber ride to airport" → Transportation
- "Starbucks coffee" → Food
- "Netflix subscription" → Entertainment
- "Salary deposit" → Income
- "CVS pharmacy" → Healthcare

Transaction: "${description}"
Category:`;
Phase 4: Frontend Implementation
UI/UX Agent Prompt:

You are a Senior Frontend Developer specialized in Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui.

Build the frontend for the Personal Finance Tracker based on the PRD and API design.

Pages/Components needed:
- Layout with navigation (authenticated vs. unauthenticated).
- Login/Signup pages (use shadcn/ui forms).
- Dashboard page: summary cards (total income, expenses, balance), charts (Recharts) for monthly overview and category breakdown.
- Transactions page: list of transactions with add/edit/delete functionality. Include a form with AI category suggestion (call the `/api/ai/suggest-category` endpoint on description blur).
- Export button to download CSV.

State management: Use React Query (TanStack Query) for server state and Zustand for client state.

Provide all components, pages, and hooks. Ensure responsive design and accessibility.
Phase 5: Integration & Polish
Integration Agent Prompt:

You are a Full-Stack Developer responsible for integrating the frontend and backend.

Tasks:
1. Connect the authentication flow: login/signup forms should call the NextAuth endpoints and redirect to dashboard.
2. Wire up the transactions API: fetch, create, update, delete.
3. Implement the AI category suggestion: on transaction description input blur, call the AI endpoint and pre-fill the category field.
4. Connect the dashboard charts to the analytics API.
5. Implement CSV export using the transaction data.

Ensure loading states, error handling, and optimistic updates for better UX.

Provide the updated frontend code and any necessary backend adjustments.
Phase 6: Testing
Test Agent Prompt:

You are a QA Automation Engineer. Write a comprehensive test suite for the Personal Finance Tracker.

Include:
1. Unit tests for critical backend functions (category suggestion logic, validation).
2. API integration tests (using Supertest or similar) for all endpoints, including authentication.
3. Frontend component tests with React Testing Library for key interactions (login, add transaction).
4. End-to-end tests with Playwright for the main user flows: signup, login, add transaction with AI suggestion, view dashboard, export CSV.

Provide the test files and instructions to run them.
Phase 7: Documentation & Deployment
Documentation Agent Prompt:

You are a Technical Writer. Create the following documentation for the Personal Finance Tracker project:

1. README.md: Project overview, features, tech stack, setup instructions (local development), environment variables, and deployment steps.
2. API Documentation: List all endpoints with request/response examples (can use OpenAPI/Swagger format).
3. User Guide: Brief instructions on how to use the app (with screenshots placeholders).

Make the documentation clear, concise, and professional.
Orchestrating the Crew with CrewAI
As Lead Developer, you can run these agents sequentially using CrewAI. Below is the orchestration code skeleton (expanded from the module outline).

# capstone_crew.py
import os
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process
from crewai_tools import FileReadTool, FileWriteTool
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

# Define tools
file_read = FileReadTool()
file_write = FileWriteTool()

# Agents
product_agent = Agent(
    role="Senior Product Manager",
    goal="Create a detailed PRD for the Personal Finance Tracker",
    backstory="Experienced PM with a focus on fintech products.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

architect_agent = Agent(
    role="Principal Architect",
    goal="Design the technical architecture and database schema",
    backstory="Seasoned architect with deep expertise in Next.js and PostgreSQL.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

backend_agent = Agent(
    role="Senior Backend Engineer",
    goal="Implement the Next.js API routes and Prisma models",
    backstory="Expert in TypeScript, Next.js, and Prisma.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

ai_agent = Agent(
    role="AI Engineer",
    goal="Implement the AI category suggestion feature",
    backstory="Specialist in integrating LLMs into applications.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

frontend_agent = Agent(
    role="Senior Frontend Developer",
    goal="Build the Next.js frontend with Tailwind and shadcn/ui",
    backstory="UI/UX expert with a keen eye for design.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

integration_agent = Agent(
    role="Full-Stack Developer",
    goal="Integrate frontend and backend, manage state and data fetching",
    backstory="Versatile developer who connects all the pieces.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

test_agent = Agent(
    role="QA Automation Engineer",
    goal="Write comprehensive tests for the application",
    backstory="Meticulous tester who leaves no edge case uncovered.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

docs_agent = Agent(
    role="Technical Writer",
    goal="Create README, API docs, and user guide",
    backstory="Clear communicator who makes complex things simple.",
    tools=[file_read, file_write],
    verbose=True
)

# Tasks (descriptions abbreviated for brevity; use full prompts from above)
task_prd = Task(description="Create PRD for Personal Finance Tracker...", agent=product_agent, expected_output="PRD.md")
task_arch = Task(description="Design architecture and DB schema...", agent=architect_agent, expected_output="Architecture.md and schema.prisma")
task_backend = Task(description="Implement backend API...", agent=backend_agent, expected_output="Backend code files")
task_ai = Task(description="Implement AI category suggestion...", agent=ai_agent, expected_output="AI integration code")
task_frontend = Task(description="Build frontend components and pages...", agent=frontend_agent, expected_output="Frontend code files")
task_integration = Task(description="Integrate frontend and backend...", agent=integration_agent, expected_output="Updated full-stack code")
task_tests = Task(description="Write test suite...", agent=test_agent, expected_output="Test files")
task_docs = Task(description="Create documentation...", agent=docs_agent, expected_output="README.md, API docs")

# Crew
crew = Crew(
    agents=[product_agent, architect_agent, backend_agent, ai_agent, frontend_agent, integration_agent, test_agent, docs_agent],
    tasks=[task_prd, task_arch, task_backend, task_ai, task_frontend, task_integration, task_tests, task_docs],
    process=Process.sequential,
    verbose=True
)

result = crew.kickoff()

# Save outputs (in practice, tasks would write to files via tools or callbacks)
print("Capstone project generation complete!")
Lead Developer Responsibilities
As the human Lead Developer, your role is critical:
<ul>
• Review Every Output: Read the PRD, architecture, code, and tests. Ensure they meet your standards and the project vision.
• Iterate with Follow-up Prompts: If an agent's output is incomplete or off-track, provide feedback in a follow-up task or manually edit.
• Make Architectural Decisions: The architect agent provides recommendations; you make the final call (e.g., choose between JWT and session cookies).
• Handle Integration Glitches: AI-generated code may have minor inconsistencies. You'll resolve merge conflicts and ensure the app runs.
• Run and Debug: Execute the code, fix any runtime errors, and verify features work as expected.
• Deploy: Push to GitHub, set up Vercel/Netlify, configure environment variables, and launch.
Final Review Checklist
Before considering the project complete, verify:
<ul>
• User can sign up and log in.
• Authenticated user can add a transaction with AI category suggestion.
• Transactions list updates and allows edit/delete.
• Dashboard displays accurate charts based on transactions.
• Export CSV downloads a valid file with transaction data.
• All tests pass (unit, integration, e2e).
• No security vulnerabilities (run Semgrep, npm audit).
• Responsive design works on mobile.
• Documentation is clear and complete.
Deployment
Deploy the application using Vercel (for Next.js) and a PostgreSQL provider like Supabase or Neon.

# Example Vercel deployment steps
1. Push code to GitHub repository.
2. Import project into Vercel.
3. Add environment variables: DATABASE_URL, OPENAI_API_KEY, NEXTAUTH_SECRET, NEXTAUTH_URL.
4. Deploy.
5. Run Prisma migrations on the production database.
Real-World Success Metrics
Developers who have completed similar AI-driven capstone projects report:
<ul>
• Project completion time reduced by 60-70% compared to manual coding.
• Higher code quality due to automated tests and security reviews by specialized agents.
• Increased confidence in tackling new tech stacks (agents handle the boilerplate).
• A portfolio piece that impresses employers and clients.
References and Further Reading
<ul>
• CrewAI Documentation: Official guides for multi-agent orchestration.
• Next.js Documentation: App Router and API routes.
• Prisma Documentation: ORM for PostgreSQL.
• shadcn/ui: Beautifully designed components.
• Recharts: Composable charting library.
• OpenAI API: Function calling guide.
• Vercel Deployment: Deploying Next.js apps.
Congratulations!
You have mastered professional AI-agent development. By completing this capstone, you've demonstrated the ability to:
<ul>
• Design and orchestrate a multi-agent AI development team.
• Craft effective prompts for each phase of the software lifecycle.
• Integrate AI capabilities (category suggestion) into a full-stack application.
• Build a production-ready web application with modern tools.
• Apply security, testing, and deployment best practices.

You can now leverage AI to 10x your productivity and build complex applications with confidence. This capstone project is a powerful addition to your portfolio. Continue experimenting, keep learning, and lead the future of AI-augmented software development.

courses.title: Prompt Engineering Foundations: Communicate Effectively with AI
courses.description: Learn to speak the language of AI. This course teaches you the fundamentals of prompt engineering-how to craft clear, effective instructions to get accurate, useful responses from Large Language Models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. No coding or AI background required.

modules.title: Module 1: What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?
content: Before crafting prompts, you need to understand what's on the other side. This module demystifies LLMs-how they are trained, what they 'know,' and why they behave the way they do.
How LLMs Work: The Simplified View

LLMs are neural networks trained on massive text datasets from the internet. They learn patterns, grammar, facts, and reasoning by predicting the next word in a sequence. Think of them as an incredibly well-read autocomplete system.
<ul>
• Training Data: Books, articles, code, websites-up to a cutoff date.
• Parameters: Billions of adjustable 'knobs' that store knowledge.
• Context Window: The amount of text the model can 'remember' in one conversation (e.g., 128k tokens for GPT-4 Turbo).
Key Concepts
<ul>
• Token: A chunk of text (word or subword). ~1 token ~= 3/4 of an English word.
• Temperature: Controls randomness. Low (0) = deterministic, High (1) = creative.
• Hallucination: When the model confidently generates false information.
Practical: Counting Tokens

# Install tiktoken (OpenAI's tokenizer)
# pip install tiktoken

import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")
tokens = enc.encode("Hello, world!")
print(f"Token count: {len(tokens)}")  # Output: 4
print(f"Tokens: {tokens}")  # [15496, 11, 1917, 0]
Key Takeaway
Understanding LLM limitations helps you write better prompts and interpret responses critically.

modules.title: Module 2: Anatomy of an Effective Prompt
content: A prompt is more than just a question. This module breaks down the components of a high-quality prompt: instruction, context, input data, and output format.
The Four Pillars of a Prompt
<ol>
• Instruction: Clear, actionable task (e.g., 'Summarize the following text').
• Context: Background information that frames the task (e.g., 'You are a legal assistant').
• Input Data: The specific content to process (e.g., the article text).
• Output Indicator: Desired format (e.g., 'Provide a bulleted list').
Bad Prompt vs. Good Prompt
Bad: 'Tell me about climate change.' (Too broad; model might ramble).
Good: 'Explain the greenhouse effect in 3 sentences suitable for a 10-year-old. Use simple analogies.'
Practical: Prompt Template Structure

template = """
[Role] Act as a {role}.
[Task] {instruction}
[Context] {context}
[Data] {input_data}
[Format] Respond in {format}.
"""

prompt = template.format(
    role="experienced chef",
    instruction="provide a recipe for a simple weeknight dinner",
    context="the user is a beginner cook with basic kitchen tools",
    input_data="available ingredients: chicken breast, rice, broccoli",
    format="a step-by-step numbered list"
)
print(prompt)
Key Takeaway
Clarity and structure are the foundations of effective prompting.

modules.title: Module 3: Role Prompting and Persona Setting
content: Assigning a role to the AI dramatically improves output quality by activating relevant knowledge pathways. This module teaches you to use 'Act as...' prompts effectively.
Why Role Prompting Works

LLMs are trained on diverse text, including conversations from specific professions. Telling the model 'You are a patent attorney' primes it to use legal terminology and logical structures typical of that domain.
Examples of Effective Personas
<ul>
• 'Act as a strict but fair copy editor.'
• 'You are a patient math tutor explaining concepts to a struggling student.'
• 'Respond as a cynical noir detective from a 1940s film.'
Practical: Comparing Responses With and Without Role

# Without Role
prompt1 = "Explain what a black hole is."

# With Role
prompt2 = """Act as an astrophysicist giving a public lecture.
Explain what a black hole is using an analogy involving a drain in a bathtub.
Keep it engaging for a non-scientific audience."""
Key Takeaway
Persona prompts guide tone, depth, and accuracy.

modules.title: Module 4: Few-Shot Prompting - Teaching by Example
content: Few-shot prompting provides the model with examples of the desired input-output pairs within the prompt itself. This is the most effective way to control output format and style without fine-tuning.
Zero-Shot vs. One-Shot vs. Few-Shot
<ul>
• Zero-Shot: No examples given.
• One-Shot: One example provided.
• Few-Shot: 2-5 examples provided.
Practical: Sentiment Classification with Few-Shot

prompt = """
Classify the sentiment of the following movie reviews as Positive or Negative.

Review: 'This movie was a masterpiece. I loved every second.'
Sentiment: Positive

Review: 'A complete waste of time. The plot was nonsensical.'
Sentiment: Negative

Review: 'It was okay. I didn't hate it, but I wouldn't watch it again.'
Sentiment: Neutral

Review: 'The acting was phenomenal and the visuals stunning.'
Sentiment: """
# The model will predict 'Positive' based on the pattern.
Key Takeaway
Show, don't just tell. Examples are the strongest constraint you can place on an LLM.

modules.title: Module 5: Controlling Output Format and Structure
content: When integrating LLMs into applications, you need structured data (JSON, XML, Markdown tables). This module covers techniques to enforce specific output schemas.
Explicit Format Instructions
Use clear, direct language: 'Respond ONLY with valid JSON. Do not include any explanation.'
Practical: Generating JSON Output

prompt = """
Extract the following information from the text below and output it as a JSON object with keys: 'name', 'age', 'occupation', 'city'.

Text: 'Sarah Chen, a 34-year-old software engineer, recently moved to Austin from Seattle.'

Output ONLY the JSON object:
"""
# Expected output: {"name": "Sarah Chen", "age": 34, "occupation": "software engineer", "city": "Austin"}
Using Markdown Tables

prompt = """
Create a comparison table of three popular programming languages (Python, JavaScript, Go).
Include columns: Language, Paradigm, Typing, Best Use Case.
Format as a Markdown table.
"""
Key Takeaway
Specify the output format explicitly, and provide an example if the structure is complex.

modules.title: Module 6: Basic Iterative Refinement of Prompts
content: Your first prompt rarely yields the perfect result. This module teaches a systematic approach to debugging and improving prompts through iteration.
The Prompt Refinement Loop
<ol>
• Analyze Failure: Why was the output wrong? Too vague? Missing context?
• Hypothesize Fix: Add constraints, clarify ambiguity, provide an example.
• Test: Run the new prompt.
• Repeat.
Practical: Iterating on a Summary Prompt

# Attempt 1 (Too long)
prompt_v1 = "Summarize this article."

# Attempt 2 (Too short, missing key details)
prompt_v2 = "Summarize this article in one sentence."

# Attempt 3 (Just right)
prompt_v3 = "Summarize the main argument of this article in 2-3 concise sentences, focusing on the author's proposed solution to climate change."
Key Takeaway
Prompt engineering is an iterative process. Don't expect perfection on the first try.

modules.title: Module 7: Managing Conversation History and Context
content: LLMs are stateless; they don't 'remember' previous conversations unless you provide the history. This module covers how to maintain context across multiple turns.
The Message Array
API calls use a list of messages with roles: 'system' (overall instructions), 'user' (your queries), 'assistant' (model responses).

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "My name is Alice."},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hello Alice, how can I help?"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "What's my name?"}
]
# The model can answer 'Alice' because it's in the history.
Context Window Limitations
If the conversation history exceeds the context window, you must truncate or summarize older messages.
Key Takeaway
Maintain state by including the full conversation history in each API call.

modules.title: Module 8: Avoiding Common Pitfalls and Hallucinations
content: Even with perfect prompts, LLMs can hallucinate or produce biased outputs. This module teaches mitigation strategies.
Reducing Hallucination
<ul>
